title: The Arithmetic of Some Dirichlet L-Values
speaker: Frank Calegari
abstract: Starting with the "Leibniz" formula for π π/4=1−1/3+1/5−1/7+… the special values of Dirichlet L-functions have long been a source of fascination and frustration. From Euler's solution in 1734 of the Basel problem to Apery's proof in 1978 that zeta(3) is irrational, our progress on understanding the arithmetic of these numbers has been limited. In this talk, we  discuss some results (old and new) about these numbers. This is joint work with Vesselin Dimitrov and Yunqing Tang.
tags: Visions in Arithmetic and Beyond: Celebrating Peter Sarnak's Work and Impact

proof_stages.label: Problem, known landscape, and main theorem
proof_stages.summary: The speaker defines the quadratic Dirichlet L-values under consideration, reviews the known irrationality and transcendence results, and states the new theorem that L(2,χ_{−3}) is irrational and even linearly independent from π².

proof_stages.label: Apéry-Siegel overconvergence template
proof_stages.summary: Apéry’s proof of the irrationality of ζ(3) is recast through recurrences, a Picard-Fuchs-type differential equation, overconvergence of a special linear combination, and LCM-controlled integrality leading to an exponential-decay contradiction.

proof_stages.label: Geometric origin of good differential equations
proof_stages.summary: The talk explains that the relevant G-functions and their good denominator behavior should come from geometry, where periods of degenerating families supply the constants that appear in overconvergent combinations.

proof_stages.label: Using analytic continuation beyond disks
proof_stages.summary: Instead of estimating only from the disk of convergence, the argument exploits continuation through larger punctured or slit regions via conformal radius, Pólya’s theorem, and André’s multivalued variant, improving the available numerical thresholds.

proof_stages.label: Denominator obstruction and holonomic replacement
proof_stages.summary: Because the functions arising in practice have LCM-type denominators rather than integer coefficients, powers destroy the denominator control, so algebraicity criteria are replaced by derivative spans and quantitative holonomicity statements.

proof_stages.label: Quantitative holonomy bounds
proof_stages.summary: The speaker states and refines holonomy theorems bounding spaces of functions with prescribed analytic continuation and denominator growth, improving a maximum to an average and then sharpening the arithmetic denominator term using high-dimensional auxiliary functions and Vandermonde factors.

proof_stages.label: Dimension contradiction for the target L-value
proof_stages.summary: The refined bounds are applied to functions on P¹ minus three points: small dimension bounds, together with modular elliptic-curve functions known from Zagier/Apéry-style sequences, give a contradiction to any forbidden rational linear relation and hence prove the new irrationality result.
